summary: Scorching Scarlet>Fire & tight jams make this Hampton show a spring tour highlight
blurb: The band delivers a fiery Scarlet Begonias → Fire on the Mountain segue and a powerful second set highlighted by Estimated Prophet and a soaring West L.A. Fadeaway solo. Brent’s keyboard scat and Mickey’s talking‑drum textures add extra color, while Jerry’s lead work shines throughout.
review: April 13, 1984 at the Hampton Coliseum opened the spring ’84 tour with a set that immediately got into gear. After a solid first set anchored by West L.A. Fadeaway and Bird Song, the second set exploded – the legendary Scarlet Begonias → Fire on the Mountain transition roared like a furnace, propelled by Jerry’s blistering lead lines and Brent’s marimba‑style keyboard textures. The band then dove into an adventurous Estimated Prophet jam that had Phil laying down funky, propulsive bass while Mickey Hart added his signature talking‑drum flavor on Fire. Highlights continued with a tight Wheel → Truckin' run, a rollicking Good Lovin’, and a rousing encore of U.S. Blues. Across every recording the show is praised for its energy, clarity, and especially Brent’s scat‑vocals/organ solo and Mickey’s inventive percussive accents, making this one of the hottest Hampton dates of the year.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: All ten recordings are overwhelmingly positive, repeatedly calling the Scarlet>Fire transition 'scorching', Brent's keyboard work 'bad ass', and Mickey's talking drum 'trippy'. Multiple sources highlight the same songs (Scarlet Begonias, Fire on the Mountain, Estimated Prophet) and praise the overall energy, indicating strong consensus. Only two recordings note mixed quality but still describe a fun, high‑energy show, so no negative sentiment outweighs the positives.

best_recording.reason: Crystal‑clear soundboard capture with excellent balance, no dropouts, and the most frequently praised audio quality across reviews.

key_highlights: Scarlet Begonias → Fire on the Mountain segue that ignites the second set
Brent Mydland’s scat vocals and marimba‑style keyboard textures
Mickey Hart’s talking‑drum moments on Fire and throughout the jam
Jerry Garcia’s soaring West L.A. Fadeaway solo and lead work on Fire

band_performance.Jerry: Shines on Fire with a heavy strum and soaring leads; delivers a standout West L.A. Fadeaway solo and vocal power throughout, though one review notes brief frustration during the Estimated Prophet jam.
band_performance.Phil: Provides funky, propulsive bass lines that lock the band together, especially evident in the Scarlet>Fire transition and the deep groove of Estimated Prophet; consistently audible in the mix.
band_performance.Bob: Adds solid rhythm guitar work and vocal contributions on Fire; while not a focus of most reviews, his presence supports the overall drive of the set.
band_performance.Brent: Delivers impressive scat vocals, organ/synth textures, and a marimba‑like keyboard approach that elevate the Scarlet>Fire transition and All Over Now; highlighted for a “bad ass” solo and vocal flair.
band_performance.Mickey: Features inventive talking‑drum work on Fire, melodic hand‑drum fills, and a trippy percussive presence that many reviewers cite as a highlight of the night.

must_listen_sequences: Goin' Down The Road Feeling Bad
